senses.g: Okara (a city in Okara district, Punjab, Pakistan)

اوکاڑہ.e: From Punjabi اوکان (okān), a type of tree.

اوکھل.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀉𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀮 (ukkhala), from Sanskrit *उदुक्खल (*udukkhala), per Turner.

اول.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَوَّل (awwal), borrowed from Arabic أَوَّل (ʔawwal).
Borrowed from Classical Persian اَوَّل (awwal), borrowed from Arabic أَوَّل (ʔawwal).

اولاد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَوْلَاد (awlād, “offspring, child(ren), descendant”), from Arabic أَوْلَاد (ʔawlād, “children”), plural of وَلَد (walad, “child; son”).

اولی.e: Borrowed from Arabic أَوْلَى (ʔawlā), elative of وَلِيّ (waliyy).

senses.g: plural of وَلی (valī) — friends (of God), saints, holy men, prophets, apostles.

اولیا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَوْلِیَاء (awliyā'), from Arabic أَوْلِيَاء (ʔawliyāʔ).

اون.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀉𑀡𑁆𑀡𑀸 (uṇṇā), from Sanskrit ऊर्णा (ū́rṇā), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hwŕ̥HnaH, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂wĺ̥h₁neh₂ (“wool”).

ex.u: ذبح کرنا گائے بکری اونٹ پالو مرغی بط کا درست ہے۔
ex.r: zibh karnā gāe bakrī ū̃ṭ pālo murġī bat kā durust hai.
ex.e: The slaughter of cow, goat, camel, domestic chicken, [and] duck is permissible.

اونٹ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi اونٿ (avnṭ /⁠ūṭ⁠⁠/) (c. 1609) / اوٿ (avṭ /⁠ūṭ⁠⁠/) (c. 1565), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀉𑀝𑁆𑀝 (uṭṭa), from Sanskrit उष्ट्र (úṣṭra, “camel”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *úštras. Doublet of شُتُر (śutur). Cognates Northwestern Indo-Aryan cognates include Punjabi اُوٹھ / ਊਠ (ūṭh), Saraiki اُٹھ (uṭh) and Sindhi اُٺُ (uṭhu). Also compare the Iranian cognates Pashto اُوښ (ūx̌) and Classical Persian اُشْتُر (uštur).

Inherited from Middle Hindi اونٿ (avnṭ /⁠ūṭ⁠⁠/) (c. 1609) / اوٿ (avṭ /⁠ūṭ⁠⁠/) (c. 1565), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀉𑀝𑁆𑀝 (uṭṭa), from Sanskrit उष्ट्र (úṣṭra, “camel”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *úštras. Doublet of شُتُر (śutur). Cognates Northwestern Indo-Aryan cognates include Punjabi اُوٹھ / ਊਠ (ūṭh), Saraiki اُٹھ (uṭh) and Sindhi اُٺُ (uṭhu). Also compare the Iranian cognates Pashto اُوښ (ūx̌) and Classical Persian اُشْتُر (uštur).

ex.u: تصویر کو تھوڑا اور اونچا کرو۔
ex.e: Move the picture a little higher.

اونچا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀉𑀘𑁆𑀘 (ucca), from Sanskrit उच्च (ucca). Doublet of اچ, a tatsama. Cognate with Punjabi ਉੱਚ (ucca) / اُچّ (ucc), Gujarati ઉંચું (uñcũ), Marathi उंच (uñca), Bengali উঁচু (ũcu), Dhivehi އުސް (us).

senses.g: A clever person; a sharp, street-smart man; a slick operator
A big or important person; an influential man; a high-ranking or wealthy individual (see also: توپ چیز)
An interesting or unusual person (see also: اونچی فلم)

اونگھ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi [script needed] (*ōṅgh), from Sanskrit अवोङ्घा (*avōṅghā).

senses.g: oh no! (an interjection used for expressing sorrow, pain or distress)

اوہ.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit ऊह (ūha), ultimately from the root ऊह् (ūh).

senses.g: Ohio (a state of the United States)
People of faith, O ye who believe (an instruction in the Qur'an)

اہل ایمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اهل ایمان (ahl-i īmān), which is a Persian variant of the Quranic verse يَا أَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (yā ʔayyuhā llaḏīna ʔāmanū, “O ye who believe”) by some Muslim scholars, especially via Persian translation of the Qur'an by Mirza Mahdi Elahi Ghomshei (1901–1973).

اہلیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَهْلِيَه, from Arabic أَهْلِيَّة (ʔahliyya). Doublet of اَہْلِیَّت (ahliyyat).

اہم.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit अहम् (aham). Related to مَیں (ma͠i).
Borrowed from Classical Persian اَهَم (aham), from Arabic أَهَمّ (ʔahamm).

ex.u: شطرنج میں اِس چال کی اہمیت کیا ہے؟
ex.r: śatrañj mẽ is cāl kī ahmiyat kyā hai?
ex.e: In chess what is the significance of this move?

ایالت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِیَالَت (iyālat), borrowed from Arabic إِيَالَة (ʔiyāla).

ایتھنز.e: Borrowed from English Athens, ultimately from Ancient Greek Ἀθηνῶν (Athēnôn).

ex.u: وقت ختم ہو جانے کی وجہ سے سوال و جواب کا موقع نہ مل سکا۔ نماز کے بعد کھانا کھایا اور پھر ایڈنبرا چل دیئے۔
ex.e: As time was running out, there was no opportunity for a question and answer session. After prayer, we had food and set off for Edinburgh.

senses.g: Iran (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)

ایزدی.e: From Persian ایزدی, from Persian noun ایزد, from Avestan yazata "worthy of reverence". Compare Sanskrit यजत (yajata).

senses.g: to make a mockery of something

ex.u: ہم روزانہ ایشور سے دعا کرتے ہیں۔
ex.r: ham rozāna īśvar se du'ā karte ha͠i.
ex.e: We pray to God every day.

senses.g: Asia (the largest continent, located between Europe and the Pacific Ocean)
Asia (an ancient province of the Roman Empire in western Anatolia, existing between 133 BC (during the Roman Republic) and the 7th century AD)

ایک.e: Inherited from Old Hindi एक (eka), from Apabhramsa एक्क (ekka), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀏𑀓𑁆𑀓 (ekka), from Sanskrit एक (éka). Doublet of یَک (yak).
Inherited from Old Hindi एक (eka), from Apabhramsa एक्क (ekka), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀏𑀓𑁆𑀓 (ekka), from Sanskrit एक (éka). Doublet of یَک (yak).

senses.g: To put on airs; to act with swagger; to try to project a fake image.

ایکشن کمار.e: From English Action + borrowed from Sanskrit कुमार (kumāra)

ایکواڈور.e: Borrowed from English Ecuador, from Spanish Ecuador.

ایکھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀇𑀓𑁆𑀔𑀼 (ikkhu), from Sanskrit इक्षु (ikṣu).

ایلچی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ایلْچِی (ēlčī), from Turkic, ultimately from Proto-Turkic *ēlči, from *ēl (“realm, country”) + *-či (profession suffix). Compare Azerbaijani elçi, Turkish elçi, Uzbek elchi and Gujarati એલચી (elcī).

senses.g: One of two prophets, the Elder and the Younger, and a book (Book of Alma) in the Book of Mormon.

ایمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِیمَان (īmān), borrowed from Arabic إِيمَان (ʔīmān), from آمَنَ (ʔāmana). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi ایمان (ayman).

senses.g: a female given name, Iman, Imaan, or Eman, from Arabic

ایمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِیمَان (īmān), borrowed from Arabic إِيمَان (ʔīmān), from آمَنَ (ʔāmana). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi ایمان (ayman).

ایماندار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ایماندار (imān-dār), from Arabic إيمان (ʔīmān).
Borrowed from Classical Persian ایماندار (imān-dār), from Arabic إيمان (ʔīmān).

اینٹ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀇𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀸 (iṭṭā), from Sanskrit इष्टा (iṣṭā), related to इष्टका (íṣṭakā, “brick”).

senses.g: Antwerp (the largest city and provincial capital of the province of Antwerp, Belgium)

ex.u: ہَمیں وِمَان اِینْدھَن اِس ہَوَائِی اَڈّے پہ نَہِیں مِلیگَا.
ex.r: hamẽ vimān īndhan is havāī aḍḍe pe nahī̃ milegā.
ex.e: We won't be able to get jet fuel at this airport.

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

ایوب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَیُّوب (ayyūb), from Arabic أَيُّوب (ʔayyūb).

اے.e: Inherited from Sanskrit हे (he), loss of 'h' via colloquial standardisation and/or Punjabi influence. Equivalent to Hindi हे (he).

senses.g: here, in this place or world

اهہ.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit इह (ihá), doublet of یَہاں (yahā̃).

senses.g: A vowel of Urdu, the variant of alif used for initial /aː/.

آب حیات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آب حیات (āb-i hayāt, literally “water of life”).

آب و ہوا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آب و هوا (āb-u-hawā, literally “water and air”).

senses.g: denotes a populated or inhabited area.

آبادی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آبادی (ābādi). Equivalent to آباد (ābād) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: Aban, the eighth month of the solar Persian calendar.

آبان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آبان (ābān), related to آب (āb, “water”).

آبشار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آبشار (ābšār), in turn derived from Middle Persian 𐭠𐭡𐭱𐭠𐭥 (ābšār). By surface analysis, آب (āb, “water”) + شار (śār, “pour”), see Persian شاریدن (šārīdan) for the second part.

آبنائے.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آبنای (ābnāy, “creek”), compound of آب (āb, “water”) + نای (nāy, “lane, throat, pipe”).

senses.g: second-person subjective or objective personal pronoun, grammatically plural: you, ye; Early Modern English formal ye.
third-person subjective or objective personal pronoun, exclusively used for the Islamic prophet Muhammad: he

ex.u: آپ ﷺ نے فرمایا "جس شخص نے نکاح کر لیا اس نے اپنا آدھا دین مکمل کر لیا"۔
ex.r: āp SAW ne farmāyā "jis śaxs ne nikāh kar liyā us ne apnā ādhā dīn mukammal kar liyā".
ex.e: He SAW said, "The person who gets married has completed half of his religion".
the machine started on its own

آپ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀧𑁆𑀧𑀸 (appā), from Sanskrit आत्मन् (ātmán, “soul, spirit; self”), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *HáHtmā, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *HáHtmā, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁éh₁t-mō ~ *h₁h₁t-m̥nés (“breath, spirit”), from *h₁eh₁t- (“to breathe”) + *-mō. Cognate with Gujarati આપું (āpũ), Nepali आंत (ānta), Marathi आपण (āpaṇ), Bengali আপনি (apni), Sinhalese අපි (api). Compare Kamkata-viri amú (“self”), Dhivehi އަހަރެން (aharen̊).

آپا.e: Borrowed from Chagatai اپا (apa, “older sister”), not to be confused with the term of Prakrit origin, perhaps a feminine derivative of, 𑀅𑀧𑁆𑀧 (appa, “father”); see Hindi आपा (āpā).

senses.g: alternative form of آپا (āpā, “older sister; sis”)
flour of unspecified kind or wholegrain

آٹا.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀅𑀝𑁆𑀝 (*aṭṭa), from Sanskrit *आ॒र्त (*ārtá), a vṛddhi derivative of *ऋ॒त (*ṛtá, “ground”), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hr̥Htás, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂l̥h₁-tós (“ground”), from *h₂elh₁-. Cognate with Classical Persian آرد (ārd, “flour”), Avestan 𐬀𐬴𐬀 (aṣ̌a, “ground”), Old Armenian աղամ (ałam, “to grind”), Ancient Greek ἀλέω (aléō, “to grind”).

آٹھ.e: Inherited from Old Hindi अठ (aṭha) (with the vowel lengthened possibly under influence of other regional forms), from Apabhramsa अट्ठ (aṭṭha), from Prakrit 𑀅𑀝𑁆𑀞 (aṭṭha), from Sanskrit अष्ट (aṣṭa).

آٹھواں.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀝𑁆𑀞𑀫 (aṭṭhama), from Sanskrit अष्टम (aṣṭamá).

senses.g: A constitutional amendment in Pakistan that grants the President the power to dismiss the Prime Minister and dissolve the National Assembly.

ex.u:
میرے ہی گھر میں موئی سوکن بنی بیٹھی تھی یہ
ناس جائے اس نگوڑی آٹھویں ترمیم کا۔
ex.e:
My own home has become a rival wife because of it
Damn that wretched Eighth Amendment.
– Inayat Ali Khan

آج.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀅𑀚𑁆𑀚 (ajja), from Sanskrit अद्य (adyá).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀅𑀚𑁆𑀚 (ajja), from Sanskrit अद्य (adyá).

آج کل.e: From آج (āj, “today”) + کَل (kal, “tomorrow; yesterday”). Compare Punjabi اَجّ کَلّ (ajj kall) / ਅੱਜਕੱਲ (ajjakalla).

آخر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آخِر (āxir), borrowed from Arabic آخِر (ʔāḵir).
Borrowed from Classical Persian آخِر (āxir), borrowed from Arabic آخِر (ʔāḵir).

آخرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آخِرَت (āxirat), from Arabic الْآخِرَة (al-ʔāḵira, “the hereafter”).

ex.u: اِس بَس کا آخِری سْٹوپ کَون سا ہَے؟
ex.r: is bas kā āxirī sṭop kaun sā hai?
ex.e: What is the last stop of this bus?

آخری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آخِرِی (āxirī), from آخِر (āxir) + ـِی (-ī). By surface analysis, آخر (āxir, “last”) + ی (ī).

senses.g: Aachen (a city in North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany)

آدا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit आर्द्रक (ārdraka), through Prakrit *𑀆𑀤𑁆𑀤𑀅 (*āddaa), from आर्द्र (ārdra, “wet”), as fresh ginger is wet. Doublet of اَدْرَک (adrak). Cognates include Bengali আদা (ada), Gujarati આદુ (ādu), Nepali अदुवा (aduwā).

آداب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آدَاب (ādāb), from Arabic آدَاب (ʔādāb).
Borrowed from Classical Persian آدَاب (ādāb), from Arabic آدَاب (ʔādāb).

آدم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آدَم (ādam), borrowed from Arabic آدَم (ʔādam), borrowed from Biblical Hebrew אָדָם (āḏā́m), from Proto-Semitic *ʔadam-.
Borrowed from Classical Persian آدَم (ādam), borrowed from Arabic آدَم (ʔādam), borrowed from Biblical Hebrew אָדָם (āḏā́m), from Proto-Semitic *ʔadam-.

آدمی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آدَمِی (ādamī), from Arabic آدَمِيّ (ʔādamiyy, “human”).

آدھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀅𑀤𑁆𑀥𑀕 (*addhaga), from Sanskrit अर्धक (ardhaka).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀅𑀤𑁆𑀥𑀕 (*addhaga), from Sanskrit अर्धक (ardhaka).

senses.g: inflection of آدھا (ādhā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of آدھا (ādhā): — oblique masculine singular/plural
autumn; the season and months of autumn

آذر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آذَر (āḏar), from Middle Persian 𐭭𐭥𐭥𐭠 (NWRA /⁠ādur⁠/)

senses.g: Azar, the ninth month of the solar Persian calendar.

آذر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آذَر (āḏar), from Middle Persian 𐭭𐭥𐭥𐭠 (NWRA /⁠ādur⁠/)

senses.g: Azerbaijan (a country in the South Caucasus in Asia and Europe)
Azerbaijan (a region in northwestern Iran, where ancient Atropatene was located, encompassing the modern provinces of Ardabil, East Azerbaijan and West Azerbaijan)

آرام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آرَام (ārām), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₁rem-.

senses.g: bedchamber; bedroom, place of rest, resthouse

آرام گاہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آرامگاه (ârâmgâh). Equivalent to آرام (ārām, “rest; repose”) + گاہ (gāh, “place; hall”).

آرزو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آرزو (ārzō) with the majhūl vowel lost (ō > ū).

senses.g: Armenia (a country in the Caucasus in Europe and Asia)

ex.u: خُدا کے فَضْل و کَرْم سے وہ اِس مَرْض سے آروگْیَہ حاصِل کِیا۔
ex.r: xudā ke fazl o karm se vo is marz se ārogya hāsil kiyā.
ex.e: By the grace of God, he attained recovery from this illness.

آری.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀆𑀭𑀸 (ārā) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from Sanskrit आरा॑ (ā́rā).

senses.g: a male given name, Aryaman, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage

آریامن.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit अर्यमन् (aryamán), with influence from آرْیَہ (ārya, “noble”). Compare Avestan 𐬀𐬌𐬭𐬌𐬌𐬀𐬨𐬀𐬥 (aⁱriiaman).

senses.g: Aryan, Indo-Iranian, the eastern branch of the Indo-European peoples

آریہ.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit आर्य (ārya). Compare Persian آریا (âriyâ, “Aryan”).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit आर्य (ārya). Compare Persian آریا (âriyâ, “Aryan”).

senses.g: Aryavarta, literally "land of the Aryans", corresponding to the northern parts of the Indian subcontinent

آریہ ورت.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit आर्यावर्त (āryāvarta, “Aryavarta”, literally “land of the Aryans”). Compare Persian ایرانویج (ērānwēj).

آڑو.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *आडु (āḍu, “peach”), a wanderwort of unknown origin. See there for more. Cognate with Classical Persian آلُو (ālū), whence Urdu آلُو (ālū). Cognate to Punjabi ਆੜੂ (āṛū), Sindhi آڙو (āṛo).

senses.g: released, freed (from enslavement or bondage etc.)
Azad Jammu and Kashmir (an autonomous territory of Pakistan; capital: Muzaffarabad)
alternative form of آزاد جموں و کشمیر (āzād jammū̃-o-kaśmīr): Azad Kashmir (an autonomous territory administered by Pakistan; in full, Azad Jammu and Kashmir)

آزادی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آزادی (āzādī). Equivalent to آزاد (āzād) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: sick, troubled, afflicted type of person

آزمانا.e: From Classical Persian آزما (āzmā) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix).

آزمودگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آزمودگی (āzmūdagī, “experience”), from the verb آزمودن (āzmūdan, “test, to experience”).

آس.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀆𑀲𑀸 (āsā), from Sanskrit आशा (āśā). Doublet of آشا (āśā).

senses.g: ease, simplicity, convenience — an instance of above; an easy thing

آسانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آسانی (āsānī). By surface analysis, آسَان (āsān) + ـِی (-ī).

senses.g: Australia (a country consisting of a main island, the island of Tasmania and other smaller islands, located in Oceania; historically, a collection of former colonies of the British Empire)
Australia (a continent consisting of the land on the Australian tectonic plate, i.e. Australia, New Guinea and intervening islands)
fond of, enamoured of, addicted to, devoted to, intent on, engrossed in
Askole (a city in Shigar district, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)
celestial sphere, firmament; one of the seven heavens

آسمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آسْمَان (āsmān). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi آسمان (āsman /⁠āsmān⁠/).

senses.g: of or relating to the sky; celestial, heavenly

آسمانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آسمانی (āsmānī). Equivalent to آسمان (āsmān, “sky, celestial sphere”) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: poetic form of آسْمَان (āsmān, “sky; heaven, skies”)
mode of sitting, posture, attitude, position

آسیب زدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آسیب زده.

آش پز خانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آشْپَزْخَانَه (āšpazxāna). Consists of آش پَز (āś paz, “chef”) + خانَہ (xāna, “place”)

آشا.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit आशा (āśā). Doublet of آس (ās).

senses.g: a female given name, Asha, from Sanskrit, of Hindu or Indian usage

آشا.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit आशा (āśā). Doublet of آس (ās).

آغا.e: From Classical Persian آغا (āġā), from Turkic. Compare Turkish ağa and Bengali আগা (aga).

آغوش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آغوش (āġōš), from Middle Persian [script needed] (ʾkwš /⁠āgōš⁠/, “embrace”).

آفت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آفَت (āfat), borrowed from Arabic آفَة (ʔāfa).

آفتاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آفْتَاب (āftāb). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi آفتاب (āftab /⁠ãftāb⁠/).

senses.g: a male given name, Aftab, from Persian, meaning “Sunshine”
a surname, Aftab, from Persian, meaning “Sunshine”

آفتاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آفْتَاب (āftāb). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi آفتاب (āftab /⁠ãftāb⁠/).

senses.g: bravo!, excellent!, good job!, well done!

آفرین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آفرین (āfarīn), from Middle Persian 𐭠𐭯𐭥𐭩𐭭 (āfrīn, “praise, blessing”).

آقا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آقا (āqā), from Turkic. Compare Turkish ağa.

آگ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀕𑁆𑀕𑀺 (aggi), from Sanskrit अग्नि (agní), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hagníš, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁n̥gʷnis. Doublet of اَگَن (agan) (semi-learned) and اَگْنِی (agnī) (learned).

آگ سے بپتسمہ.e: From the Urdu Bible, Matthew 3:11, John the Baptist said: : وہ تو تم کو رُوح القُدس اور آگ سے بپتسمہ دے گا۔ voh to tum ko rūh-ul-qūds aur āg se baptismā de gā. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire.

senses.g: building or premises devoted to a particular purpose

آگے.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Apabhramsa अग्गइ (aggaï), from Sanskrit अग्रके (agrake).

آلس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀆𑀮𑀲𑁆𑀲 (ālassa), from Sanskrit आलस्य (ā́lasya). Cognate with Assamese এলাহ (elah) and Marathi आळस (āḷas).

آلنگن.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit आलिङ्गन (āliṅgana). Compare Gujarati આલિંગન (āliṅgan), Marathi आलिंगन (āliṅgan), Kannada ಆಲಿಂಗನ (āliṅgana), Telugu ఆలింగనము (āliṅganamu), Malayalam ആലിംഗനം (āliṅganaṁ), Maithili आलिङ्गन (āliṅgan) / 𑒂𑒪𑒱𑒓𑓂𑒑𑒢 (āliṅgana), Bengali আলিঙ্গন (aliṅgon), Assamese আলিঙ্গন (aliṅgon), Sinhalese ආලිංගනය (āliṁganaya).

senses.g: having a fat body, plump, clumsy

آلو.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀆𑀮𑀼𑀕 (āluga), from Sanskrit आलुक (āluka, “elephant foot yam”). The word began to be used in the sense "potato" after the importation of potatoes to South Asia from the Americas by Europeans, displacing cultivation of the similar elephant foot yam.

آلو بخارا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian آلوی بخارا.

ex.u: سیاسی معاشرہ میں اس فیصلہ کی کافی آلوچنا ہوئی.
ex.e: This decision is heavily criticized in political symposium.
